merged.chapter_summary: Chapter 7 presents a comparative case study between France and the United States, arguing that France's 1989 educational reforms replicated the earlier American mistake of adopting individualized, skill-based instruction. The author contends that this shift caused France to fall from being one of the world's most equitable and high-performing systems to one of the most unequal and mediocre, mirroring the American decline of the 1960s and 1970s.

claims.text: The educational decline of France following the 1989 reforms constitutes a 'misunderstood tragedy' and a 'débâcle' analogous to the French military defeat of 1940.
claims.source_passage: Lafforgue... His view, which I agree with, is that top French intellectuals made big avoidable mistakes in 1989, just as higher-ups had made serious, avoidable military mistakes in 1940.

claims.text: France's educational system declined from being among the best and most equitable in the world to being one of the worst and least equitable after adopting individualized, skill-based principles.
claims.source_passage: The data showed another educational system that, like our own, had adopted skill-based, individualized, educational principles, and subsequently declined from being among the best and most equitable large school systems in the world to being one of the worst and least equitable.

claims.text: The decline of student verbal achievement in France closely mirrors the fall of verbal scores in the United States during the 1960s and 1970s.
claims.source_passage: I reached that conclusion when I found that the decline of student verbal achievement in France closely mirrored the infamous fall of verbal scores in the United States in the 1960s and 1970s.

claims.text: The American educational decline of the 1960s and 1970s was caused by progressive curriculum reforms adopted in the 1930s and 1940s.
claims.source_passage: If we insist that the nationwide progressive 'reforms' of the curriculum in the 1930s and 1940s did not chiefly cause that disaster, we simply protect the faulty ideas that are the key to the whole conundrum.

claims.text: The decline in American test scores between 1965 and 1980 affected all students, regardless of socioeconomic status.
claims.source_passage: Scholars have now shown that there was a significant score drop on virtually all US school tests—not just on the SAT—by all students, rich and poor, during the period 1965–1980.

claims.text: SAT verbal scores in the United States dropped by half a standard deviation between 1960 and 1980.
claims.source_passage: SAT verbal scores had plummeted half a standard deviation in the twenty years between 1960 and 1980.

claims.text: State tests in Iowa and Minnesota provide the most persuasive evidence that demographic changes did not cause the American score decline.
claims.source_passage: Particularly persuasive are the state tests in Iowa and Minnesota, administered to virtually all high school juniors and seniors in those states at a time when their pupil demographics were stable—thus eliminating demographic change as a cause of the declines in those states.

claims.text: Providential individualism acts like a virus that crosses national boundaries and political parties, infecting educational systems globally.
claims.source_passage: Like a virus, providential individualism knows no national boundary or political party. As I will indicate in this chapter, the virus has also infected Britain and Sweden, and until recently Germany.

claims.text: The 1989 French law (loi Jospin) led to the abandonment of the national curriculum in favor of localized and individualized topics.
claims.source_passage: The decline did not pick up speed, approaching that of the United States, until after the loi Jospin abandoned the national curriculum, and deliberately localized and individualized the topics studied in elementary school.

claims.text: Americans should import the scientific knowledge regarding the cause and cure of educational decline discovered in France.
claims.source_passage: The cause of the disease has now been definitively determined in Paris—and the cure as well. We should not hesitate to import that knowledge.

claims.text: NAEP verbal scores for American seventeen-year-olds have remained flat since 1971, which hides the decline that occurred in the decade prior.
claims.source_passage: According to the National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) the verbal scores of our seventeen-year-olds have stayed flat ever since 1971, the year we first began national sampling, a decade after our decline was well under way.

claims.text: American schools are currently infected by a system of ideas that prevents educational improvement from taking hold.
claims.source_passage: Our schools have long been infected by a system of ideas that prevents improvement from taking hold.

claims.text: Germany served as the nineteenth-century nursery of progressive education and has decisively influenced American educational policy.
claims.source_passage: Germany, which was the nineteenth-century nursery of progressive education, and has had a decisive influence on educational thought and policy in the United States.

claims.text: France has completed a valid natural experiment regarding educational outcomes for ten-year-old students over the last twenty years.
claims.source_passage: France, with its excellent record keeping, has in effect recently com- pleted an unintended natural experiment with ten-year-old students over a period of twenty years.

claims.text: French statistical and sociological educational analysis is equivalent in quality to the American National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP).
claims.source_passage: The statistical and sociological analysis is on a par with our research gold standard—the National Assessment of Educational Progress.

claims.text: American ten-year-olds do not differ fundamentally from French ten-year-olds in their potential for math and reading proficiency.
claims.source_passage: We are not so imbued with American exceptionalism as to believe that American ten-year-olds are fundamentally different from French ones with respect to math and reading proficiency.

claims.text: In the 1980s, France and Sweden possessed the most effective large school systems in Europe.
claims.source_passage: In the 1980s, France could celebrate (with Sweden) the best large school systems in Europe.

claims.text: The presence of peasant children in universities in the 1950s and 1960s proves the old French republican school system was successfully enabling social mobility.
claims.source_passage: What The Inheritors showed unwittingly was that the French republican school of the 1950s and 1960s had been doing an excellent job of bringing children of peasants to the university, thus enabling Bourdieu to study their attitudes.

claims.text: Specific grade-by-grade topics in a national curriculum incentivize publishers to produce higher quality learning materials.
claims.source_passage: The advantages of definite grade-by-grade topics for inducing publishers to produce excellent learning materials were clear.

claims.text: Educational equity and achievement are maximized when publishers compete on presentation and price rather than determining core content.
claims.source_passage: The publishers had no control over the basic content of schooling; they gained their competitive advantage by presenting the content attractively and effectively at a good price.

claims.text: In the 1980s, France was more successful at educating immigrants than any other European nation.
claims.source_passage: It educated immigrants better than any other European nation.

claims.text: The success of the pre-1989 French system was caused by near-universal preschool attendance and a highly communal common curriculum.
claims.source_passage: I had long attributed this earlier French accomplishment in overcoming inequality to the excellent French preschool system being attended by nearly 100 percent of four- and five-year- olds, and also to the fact that after preschool, children entered a highly communal primary school with a well-developed common curriculum.

claims.text: The 1989 Loi Jospin reform in France was an educational catastrophe.
claims.source_passage: the intellectual preparation for the catastrophic loi Jospin of 1989 had been brewing in French education schools since the 1960s.

claims.text: France's 1989 decentralization was implemented in a centralized, universal, and sudden manner.
claims.source_passage: Because French schooling was centrally controlled, it decentralized itself in 1989 in a wrenching, centralized manner. The suddenness and universality of its curricular changes enable us now to make some confident inferences

claims.text: The suddenness and universality of France's 1989 curricular changes allow for confident inferences about educational outcomes that are impossible in the United States.
claims.source_passage: The suddenness and universality of its curricular changes enable us now to make some confident inferences of the sort that are impossible in the United States, where specific curricular content over time is largely unspecified and unknown.

claims.text: The traditional goal of French schooling from 1790 to 1977 was to enable all children to acquire the same knowledge to unify society and reduce inequality.
claims.source_passage: I mentioned in the prologue that the common curriculum of the French primary school was a recurrent theme of French education from Condorcet in 1790 to President Giscard d’Estaing in 1977, who stated the basic idea of French schooling: to enable all French children to acquire the same knowledge in order to unify society and reduce inequality.

claims.text: The 1989 'loi Jospin' effectively overthrew the French tradition of a common curriculum by individualizing schooling for every child.
claims.source_passage: But just twelve years later, in 1989, France completely overthrew that tradition by passing a law saying in effect that each child would go to a different primary school and middle school.

claims.text: The 1989 reform required schools to prioritize a child's home culture and individual characteristics over a shared national culture.
claims.source_passage: Instead of acquiring the same culture, each school was to respect and develop the child’s home culture and conform to the individual characteristics of each locality and to the individual characteristics of each child.

claims.text: The French national curriculum was criticized by scholars like Pierre Bourdieu for sustaining elite culture and excluding children from non-elite backgrounds.
claims.source_passage: Pierre Bourdieu had been chosen to head the committee (which also included Jacques Derrida) because he had been in the forefront of scholars who complained that the old-fashioned national curriculum was not as egalitarian as it claimed to be. Rather, he said, it sustained a culture that the well-off already possessed, and thus it excluded children who came from other cultures.

claims.text: The Jospin law decentralized French education, dividing the nation into twenty-six autonomous school districts.
claims.source_passage: Structurally, a radical feature of the new law was decentralization. Instead of having everything emanate from Paris, metropolitan France would henceforth be divided into twenty-six autonomous school districts.

claims.text: The new education colleges established by the Jospin law were designed to train teachers in progressive educational theory.
claims.qualifier: Hirsch interprets 'scientific and orderly pedagogy' as a translation for progressive theory
claims.source_passage: The Jospin law also included the institution of twenty-eight new education colleges—one in each district and two extra ones in the district of Paris to train teachers in appropriate methods for the new system. They were to codify the profession of pedagogy in a scientific and orderly way (translation: they would teach progressive theory).

claims.text: The national standards (the 'socle') established after 1989 were too nonspecific to provide a practical commonality of knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Now known as “the platform” (socle), the standards document was astonishingly short and nonspecific—nonexistent in practical terms.

claims.text: The Bourdieu-Gros Report and the subsequent reform prioritized general skills over the acquisition of specific knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The emphasis was to be on skills, not facts, based on the statement in the Bourdieu-Gros Report that all-purpose skills are far more important than the piling up of knowledge.

claims.text: The preamble of the 1989 law explicitly granted students the right to elaborate their own curricula based on personal aspirations.
claims.source_passage: The law begins: “Education is the first national priority. It shall be designed and organized for the benefit of pupils and students . . . Everyone is guaranteed the right to an education, to develop his personality . . . Pupils and students will elaborate their own curricula according to their own aspirations and capacities.”

claims.text: The French Ministry of Education continues to advocate for school-level individualism despite a historical period of educational decline.
claims.source_passage: The schools were commanded to adopt individualism by taking “into account the plurality and diversity of aptitudes of each pupil” (an instruction the French Ministry of Education still gives to schools today, even after many years of decline).

claims.text: The 1989 Bourdieu-Gros Report claimed that all-purpose skills are far more important than the accumulation of knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Gros Report that all-purpose skills are far more important than the piling up of knowledge.

claims.text: The 1989 French education law shifted the system from a 'lockstep' model to a child-centered model.
claims.source_passage: The very preamble of the new 1989 law made clear that French education was not going to be “lockstep” any longer. It was going to be child-centered.

claims.text: Under the 1989 reform, each individual primary school was mandated to produce its own curriculum.
claims.source_passage: Thenceforth each primary school was to produce its own curriculum.

claims.text: Two main intellectual impulses drove French educators to demand radical change: progressive teacher training and the sociology of Pierre Bourdieu.
claims.source_passage: There were two main intellectual impulses that led educators to accept and even demand a radical change in the school curriculum.

claims.text: New French teachers trained in the 1960s were indoctrinated in 'la nouvelle éducation' (progressive education) at teacher training schools (écoles normales).
claims.source_passage: They had been instructed, during their training at the écoles normales, in a new point of view—that of la nouvelle education, which we call “progressive education.”

claims.text: Progressive education in France encouraged naturalistic teaching and the de-emphasis of memorization and grammar.
claims.source_passage: It encouraged the naturalistic teaching of math and reading. It deemphasized memorization and grammar.

claims.text: The adoption of progressive attitudes changed the methods of teaching reading, writing, and arithmetic from 'artificial imposition' to 'natural development.'
claims.source_passage: No longer were they to be learned by artificial impositions and rote memorization. They were to be developed naturally.

claims.text: Teacher unions supported the 1989 legislation because they blamed the rise in reading failure on the rigorous old curriculum.
claims.source_passage: The rise of this tradition among young French teachers encouraged the teacher union to support the radical changes that were proposed in the new 1989 legislation.

claims.text: The increase in French students failing to read and reckon in the 1970s and 80s was a consequence of new methods, not the old curriculum.
claims.source_passage: It had not been understood that the rise in the number of students who were unable to read and reckon was a consequence of the new methods, not the old ones!

claims.text: Starting in the 1960s, French education was infiltrated by the 'disastrous' theories of naturalism, individualism, and skill-centrism.
claims.source_passage: For, starting in the 1960s, under the radar, traditional teaching methods of reading and arithmetic began to give way to the three disastrous pedagogical theories identified in this book: naturalism, individualism, and skill-centrism.

claims.text: Naturalistic reading methods like 'la méthode globale' caused basic reading difficulties among young French pupils.
claims.source_passage: The new naturalistic theories into which French teachers were being indoctrinated—for example, in la méthode globale, and la méthode mixte in reading—had begun to cause basic reading difficulties among the youngest pupils

claims.text: Failures caused by progressive methods were paradoxically used as evidence to argue against the old curriculum's uniformity.
claims.source_passage: The rise in grade retentions, caused by the new methods, was then deployed successfully as a further argument against the old pedagogy and the old uniformity of the topics in the national curriculum.

claims.text: Pierre Bourdieu's 1964 book 'The Inheritors' argued that French schooling perpetuated class divisions and made a myth of equality of opportunity.
claims.source_passage: In this influential book Bourdieu and his coauthor Jean-Claude Passeron claimed that French schooling was not as egalitarian as it conceived itself to be. On the contrary, they said, it perpetuated class divisions and repressions.

claims.text: Bourdieu concluded that the French school system was an unwitting conspiracy to keep the underclasses submerged.
claims.source_passage: The French school system was unwittingly part of a conspiracy to keep the underclasses submerged.

claims.text: Bourdieu's analysis in 'The Inheritors' is superficial and exaggerates minor statistical disparities.
claims.source_passage: To be an American reading The Inheritors is to be astonished at its superficiality, its indignant making of mountains out of molehills.

claims.text: Bourdieu’s 'The Inheritors' concluded that French republican education was a device for reproducing existing social stratifications rather than overcoming them.
claims.source_passage: They concluded that French republican education was a device for reproducing existing social stratifications. The French school system was unwittingly part of a conspiracy to keep the underclasses submerged. His radical claim: French schooling was a means of reproducing social class, not overcoming it.

claims.text: Bourdieu's analysis of social-class-based preferences among university students was superficial because it only examined a single generation.
claims.source_passage: To be an American reading The Inheritors is to be astonished at its superficiality, its indignant making of mountains out of molehills. The book embraces far too narrow a time slice in describing the social-class-based preferences of university students—merely a single generation.

claims.text: The old French centralized common curriculum was a product of the egalitarian left rather than right-wing politics.
claims.source_passage: The old French centralized system with a common curriculum was not, as many on the French left imagined, a product of hidebound right-wing politics, but of the old egalitarian left, starting with Condorcet’s plan of 1790, A Common Education for Children, and Napoleon’s meritocratic principle of 'a career open to talents.'

claims.text: The Bourdieu-Gros Report of 1989 was strikingly vague regarding specific curriculum content.
claims.source_passage: Yet to read the Bourdieu-Gros Report, which became the basis for new 1989 law, is to be struck by its vagueness regarding the curriculum.

claims.text: The growth of total knowledge in the modern world makes an 'encyclopedic' or fact-heavy curriculum impossible and vain.
claims.qualifier: Hirsch attributes this claim to the Bourdieu-Gros Report while disagreeing with its implications
claims.source_passage: The new emphasis was to be on skills. It looked with disdain upon mere facts, or 'encylopedism.' It stated: 'The growth of knowledge renders vain the ambition of encyclopedism.'

claims.text: The Bourdieu-Gros Report falsely assumed that critical-thinking habits and logical techniques are general competences that transfer across domains like text reading.
claims.source_passage: It stated: 'Students are to be taught “elementary logic,” and, by the acquisition of habits of thought, the techniques and cognitive tools which are indispensable in conducting reasoning that is rigorous and thoughtful—those same general competences are required in the reading of texts.' That statement, assumed to be self-evident, is not backed up by any footnotes. Its assumptions are incorrect

claims.text: The intellectual origins of the French educational decline were identical to those that caused the decline in the United States: naturalism, individualism, and skill-centrism.
claims.source_passage: Thus the intellectual origins of the French “debacle” were largely the same theories as the ones that created “a nation at risk” in the United States: the theory that schooling should be natural, and that the aim of schooling should be the development of individuality and critical-thinking skills.

claims.text: The rise in grade repetitions in France was caused by naturalistic approaches to reading and math rather than the traditional 'lockstep' education system.
claims.source_passage: Only years later did it become clear that the alarming rise in grade repetitions had not been caused by the old education at all, but was a result of the new naturalistic approach to reading and math.

claims.text: The adoption of child-centered, individualized methods in France intensified social stratification and inequality instead of reducing it.
claims.source_passage: The new methods had not reduced social stratification and inequality but instead had intensified them.

claims.text: The French Ministry of Education soundings from 1987 to 2007 constitute the most definitive comparative study of curriculum effects ever undertaken in any country.
claims.source_passage: The results are decisive, and constitute the most definitive comparative study of curriculum effects ever undertaken in any country.

claims.text: The implementation of a child-centered skills curriculum caused average literacy scores in France to fall by four-tenths of a standard deviation over twenty years.
claims.source_passage: The child-centered skills curriculum had caused the average of literacy scores to fall four-tenths of a standard deviation.

claims.text: The decline in literacy scores was more severe among the neediest students because they lack access to academic knowledge at home.
claims.source_passage: Among the neediest students, the decline was severe... It suggests the positive equity effects of a common grade-by-grade curriculum in the elementary school when the lines are traced backward in time.

claims.text: Literacy test performance is a direct function of whether a student has acquired the knowledge and vocabulary of the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: If students gain the knowledge and vocabulary of the public sphere, they will score well on a literacy test.

claims.text: The 1990 French educational reforms systematically deprived poor children of the enabling knowledge that wealthy children acquired from their home environments.
claims.source_passage: They systematically deprived poor children of the enabling knowledge that rich children had acquired from their home environments.

claims.text: A specific, cumulative common-core curriculum is superior to an American-style individualized curriculum for achieving high and equitable outcomes.
claims.source_passage: The French experiment... has pretty well proved that a specific, cumulative common-core curriculum... is far superior to the American-style individualized curriculum.

claims.text: In mathematics, the decline in French student performance affected all demographic groups equally, whereas in language, the decline was stratified by class.
claims.source_passage: In that subject [math], all groups declined greatly, but in tandem. In language the decline was much greater among the poor than the rich.

claims.text: Placing home culture on an equal plane with the national public sphere in schools is a 'suicidal move' for national education systems.
claims.source_passage: So one aim of Bourduieu-Gros was anti–cultural imperialism, placing the home culture on a nearly equal plane with the national public sphere—a suicidal move for national schools.

claims.text: The 1990 French educational arrangements intensified the social reproduction of stratification they were intended to diminish.
claims.source_passage: As the French data from 1987 to 2007 show, the new 1990 arrangements greatly intensified the social reproduction they were supposed to diminish.

claims.text: French educational data is more significant for American education than any data collected from the American system because of its scientific precision regarding curriculum.
claims.source_passage: From a scientific standpoint, the data from France are more significant for American education than any data we have ever managed to collect from our own system.

claims.text: Teacher quality remained constant in France during the period of educational decline.
claims.source_passage: “Teacher quality” remained constant in France by all objective measures during the period of decline.

claims.text: The United States lacks large-scale, curriculum-related data of the same precision as the French data.
claims.source_passage: These findings are important for us because we lack large-scale, curriculum-related data in the United States.

claims.text: Any U.S. school district that implements a communal, cumulative curriculum in all subjects will show higher achievement and equity than under the skills-based paradigm.
claims.source_passage: These data make it nearly certain that any district in the United States that implemented a communal, cumulative curriculum in all subjects... will show higher achievement and much higher equity than it has shown in the past under the reign of the skills delusion.

claims.text: The results of the French educational shift are so negative that continuing the experiment would be considered unethical in medical research.
claims.source_passage: If these trials had occurred in medical research... it would be considered unethical to continue them.

claims.text: French teacher-training institutes became dominated by an ideological conformity that emphasizes students 'constructing their own learnings.'
claims.source_passage: Above all, those learners “were to be motivated to construct their own learnings.”

claims.text: Naturalistic methods like 'whole language' or 'balanced literacy' are less effective for teaching reading than explicit phonics instruction.
claims.source_passage: After graduation, she secretly taught her students to read via the phonics method, and they performed above the norm.

claims.text: The educational methods currently prevalent in U.S. colleges of education are effectively the same as those that caused the French decline.
claims.source_passage: American educators and policy makers will immediately recognize the French version of ideas that prevail in our own colleges of education.

claims.text: The methods and attitudes of progressive education were dominant in French teacher-training schools for over twenty years before 2004.
claims.source_passage: From his book’s opening pages he made clear that the methods and attitudes Boutonnet described were already dominant in education schools more than twenty years before.

claims.text: Students taught with traditional methods like dictation and phonics perform better than those taught with progressive 'natural' methods.
claims.source_passage: Yet students of older teachers, who dared to continue to use dictations and learn reading by phonics, fared better.

claims.text: Modern educational methods serve to justify the abandonment of academic ambitions for children.
claims.source_passage: Modern education only serves to justify the abandonment of ambitions that we have for our children.

claims.text: The primary cause of the French school crisis is the disintegration of the curriculum, particularly in primary schools.
claims.source_passage: We situate the main origin of the school crisis in the disintegration of the curriculum—principally the primary school curriculum.

claims.text: The disintegration of the curriculum has made the subject matters taught in French schools random.
claims.source_passage: this curricular disintegration has rendered more or less random the subject matters taught.

claims.text: A rich, coherent, and cumulative primary curriculum is the necessary condition for a true democratization of education.
claims.source_passage: In consequence, we see the reconstruction of the primary curriculum into one that is rich, coherent, and cumulative as the necessary condition for a true democratization of education.

claims.text: Between 1987 and 2012, France deteriorated more than any other country in the world regarding the equity of its schools.
claims.source_passage: the most recent of which, in 2012, showed that France, of all countries in the world, has deteriorated the most in the equity of its schools.

claims.text: Structural adjustments to French law have failed to rationalize the elementary curriculum because they do not address content specifics.
claims.source_passage: New adjustments to the loi Jospin have been enacted, which change structural elements at the edges, but do not rationalize the elementary curriculum.

claims.text: The 2005 law creating a 'socle' (base) of knowledge has been essentially useless because the standards remained vague and lacked specifics.
claims.source_passage: the resulting document did not get into specifics, and has been essentially useless.

claims.text: The French educational system has become the most inegalitarian in Europe due to its condemnation of cultural transmission.
claims.source_passage: Fifty years after The Inheritors, our educational system, greatly influenced by that book’s condemnation of transmission, has become the most inegalitarian in Europe.

claims.text: In a system without a shared curriculum, academic success is reserved for those whose parents know the 'tactics' to navigate the school system.
claims.source_passage: The only survivors of the shipwreck of our public education are those whose parents know the tactics for escaping the disaster—which quartiers to live in, which subjects to emphasize, which options to choose.

claims.text: Children of immigrant families are the primary victims of the decision to renounce teaching the national culture.
claims.source_passage: On the other end of the spectrum are the designated victims of social selection—among them, the children of immigrant families. It is with them in mind that we renounced teaching the culture of our country and transmitting to all a common heritage.

claims.text: The ideologies of decentralization, individualism, and the 'skills delusion' are supported by both the political left and the political right.
claims.source_passage: Bourdieu and the political left cannot boast sole ownership of decentralization, individualism, and the skills delusion. Sweden now offers a similarly compelling example from the right.

claims.text: Sweden's 1994 educational reforms were driven by a belief in the 'providential impulses' of the individual child and the 'providential workings' of the free market.
claims.source_passage: Behind this move lay the same kind of thinking about following the providential impulses of the individual child, to which Sweden added the further idea of following the providential workings of the free market.

claims.text: The decentralization of Swedish schools caused a severe decline in students' proficiency in reading, writing, and arithmetic.
claims.source_passage: He showed that decentralization had caused a severe drop in students’ abilities in reading, writing, and arithmetic.

claims.text: Decentralization in Sweden greatly widened the achievement gaps between rich and poor students.
claims.source_passage: It had greatly widened the achievement gaps between rich and poor students.

claims.text: Prior to 1994, the Swedish school system was one of the most egalitarian in the world with minor performance gaps based on socio-economic status or location.
claims.source_passage: Before the changes occurred, Lewin showed, Swedish schools exhibited only minor gaps between rich and poor students, and Sweden boasted one of the most egalitarian systems in the world.

claims.text: Under the 1994 reforms, the Swedish state largely abandoned the determination of specific knowledge content for schools.
claims.source_passage: Under the new curricula, the state largely abandoned the ambition to determine the knowledge content of the school, and transferred that determination to local schools.

claims.text: The 1994 Swedish curricula replaced detailed topic and time allocations with vague minimum requirements.
claims.source_passage: The adaptation of the 1994 curricula to the new school governance meant that the previously detailed time allocations and topics were replaced by vague time allocations that required only a minimum guaranteed teaching time.

claims.text: The Swedish decline in PISA scores between 2000 and 2012 is the largest ever recorded by PISA.
claims.source_passage: It is the largest drop ever recorded by PISA.

claims.text: Abandoning a common-core curriculum in favor of an individualistic approach is a significant policy failure for both Sweden and as a lesson for the United States.
claims.source_passage: The magnitude of the Swedish decline makes the change from a common-core curriculum throughout Sweden to an individualistic approach almost as significant for American policy as the French decline

claims.text: High equity and achievement in Sweden prior to 1994 were the result of intentional national policy centered on a common curriculum.
claims.source_passage: Before 1994, high equity and achievement in Sweden had been no accident. It had been a chief aim of national policy.

claims.text: The pre-1994 Swedish system, characterized by 'equality-oriented centralism,' resulted in small achievement differences between different schools.
claims.source_passage: Sweden followed what he calls ‘equality-oriented centralism,’ with extra resources allocated to low achieving students. Under the Swedish system, as it evolved, ‘the differences in achievement between different schools were rather small.’

claims.text: Replacing a common curriculum with the principle of 'equivalence' (individualized adaptation) leads to educational failure.
claims.source_passage: The new law of 1994 discarded those arrangements. A principle of “equivalence” was legislated to replace the common curriculum

claims.text: The refusal to transmit a common national heritage to immigrant children results in severe academic failure and potential social unrest.
claims.source_passage: Who can fail to see that that is the true violence; that such denial of transmission cannot fail, one day, to explode?

claims.text: Before 1994, every Swedish child studied the same subjects under a common national curriculum in neighborhood elementary schools.
claims.source_passage: Every Swedish child was required to attend the neighborhood elementary school, and to study the same subjects in the same classrooms under a common national curriculum, as in France.

claims.text: The 1994 Swedish law replaced the common curriculum with a principle of 'equivalence' that adapted education to each pupil's unique circumstances.
claims.source_passage: A principle of “equivalence” was legislated to replace the common curriculum... Education should be adapted to each pupil’s circumstances and needs, based on the pupils’ background, earlier experiences, language, and knowledge.

claims.text: The Swedish reform assumed that thinking skills could serve as a universal yardstick even when students were learning different, individually adapted content.
claims.source_passage: All pupils will be taught different, individually adapted topics—but all will be taught the same thinking skills: ‘Students should train themselves to think critically, to examine the facts and circumstances and to realize the consequences of different options.’

claims.text: The shift toward individuality and thinking skills caused the Swedish elementary curriculum to become incoherent.
claims.source_passage: With the new emphasis on individuality and thinking skills, the elementary ­curriculum in Sweden became as incoherent as the curriculum had become in France and the United States.

claims.text: France and Sweden destroyed effective school systems because they wrongly believed that individualization and skills-focus would improve already successful systems.
claims.source_passage: Why did France and Sweden, which stood at the top in equity and achievement, suddenly destroy two of the most admirable and effective large school systems in the world? It was because they intended to make their good systems even better.

claims.text: Sweden is second only to France in the deterioration of social equality as measured by PISA studies.
claims.source_passage: Now in the PISA studies Sweden is second only to France as the country that has deteriorated the most in social equality.

claims.text: Germany significantly improved its PISA standings in both achievement and equity by adopting well-defined curricula.
claims.source_passage: Germany is an object lesson in the other direction. It has greatly improved its PISA standings in both achievement and equity... Reacting to the Schock, Germany began to adopt well-defined curricula.

claims.text: Grade-by-grade specific curricula improve learning by clarifying exactly what teachers should teach and what students should learn.
claims.source_passage: The teachers know specifically what to teach, and the students know what they have to learn.

claims.text: The educational decline in Sweden and France cannot be attributed to a specific side of the political spectrum.
claims.source_passage: Sweden offers dramatic proof that left-versus-right ideological simplifications regarding the declines there and in France are not warranted.

claims.text: In Sweden, the movement toward individualism and skills-centrism was driven by the political Right.
claims.source_passage: In Sweden, the disastrous movement toward individualism and skills that destroyed its education came from the political right, not the left as in France.

claims.text: The political Right in Sweden and the socialist Left in France held identical positions regarding the need to adapt schooling to individual pupil diversity.
claims.source_passage: That statement from the right was exactly the position and almost the wording of the socialist left in France, which stated: ‘The school shall take into account the plurality and diversity of aptitudes of each pupil.’

claims.text: Providential individualism is rooted in a naive faith that natural development, whether in markets or children, leads to the best outcomes.
claims.source_passage: Providential individualism... captures the naïve faith by the left and the right that things will work out well if they are allowed to follow their natural courses, as if guided by the hand of Providence.

claims.text: The UK's 1967 Plowden Report encouraged progressive education with disastrous results for British schools.
claims.source_passage: In 1967, before the Bourdieu-Gros Report of 1989, Britain had issued the Plowden Report under the auspices of a left-leaning Labor government. Progressive education... now began [to be] encouraged... with disastrous results.

claims.text: In Sweden, the push for individualized education originated from the political right, whereas in France it came from the political left.
claims.source_passage: The theory offered in Sweden was that individual schools and students, left on their own without state interference, would yield better results than state-imposed commonality... That statement from the right was exactly the position and almost the wording of the socialist left in France.

claims.text: The educational positions of the Swedish political right and the French socialist left regarding individualization were virtually identical in their rhetoric.
claims.source_passage: That statement from the right was exactly the position and almost the wording of the socialist left in France, which stated: 'The school shall take into account the plurality and diversity of aptitudes of each pupil.'

claims.text: The Plowden Report (1967) institutionalized progressive education as official left-wing doctrine in the United Kingdom.
claims.source_passage: Progressive education, long an undercurrent in teacher training in the United Kingdom, now began to be institutionalized as official left-wing doctrine.

claims.text: The recommendations of the British Plowden Report closely mirrored the French Jospin reforms in their focus on the individual and 'discovery' learning.
claims.source_passage: Stipulations of the Plowden Report were very Jospin-like: the involvement of parents, children finding things out for themselves, and above all, a focus on the individual.

claims.text: The lack of detailed historical data in Britain makes it impossible to definitively prove the causal link between individualized education and widening achievement gaps there.
claims.source_passage: Lacking detailed data in Britain, it’s impossible to state with confidence the connection of individualized education with the disturbing widening of gaps in Britain.

claims.text: The 1976 Bennett Report found that progressive teaching methods in Britain exacerbated inequalities without improving student creativity.
claims.source_passage: The Bennett Report of 1976 argued that the data showed that the new methods were exacerbating inequalities without leading to any improvement in 'creativity.'

claims.text: Modern British teacher unions advocate for 'personalization' because their members have been indoctrinated with progressive ideology in teacher-training institutions.
claims.source_passage: Their membership has simply been indoctrinated by their teacher-training institutions, which are theological institutes for progressivism.

claims.text: Historically, the political left favored educational universalism—offering the same opportunity to all children—as seen in the early work of Condorcet and Jefferson.
claims.source_passage: Ever since Condorcet and Jefferson, the left has favored universalism—that is, the idea of offering each child the very same educational opportunities in the early years.

claims.text: Educational individualism and the 'skills delusion' have no inherent logical connection to either the political left or right.
claims.source_passage: Educational individualism and the skills delusion have no inherent connection with either the left or right.

claims.text: The primary weakness of progressive education is its failure to provide a theory of social welfare beyond extreme individualism or anarchy.
claims.source_passage: The great weakness of Progressive Education lies in the fact that it has elaborated no theory of social welfare, unless it be that of anarchy or extreme individualism.

claims.text: To achieve power, poor children must master existing intellectual tools rather than attempting to 'invent' their own.
claims.source_passage: With his unerring penetration, he [Gramsci] understood that poor children needed to master the intellectual tools of power rather than implausibly trying to invent their own.

claims.text: Cognitive science demonstrates that broad, shared knowledge is a prerequisite for effective language use.
claims.source_passage: cognitive science clearly indicates that broad, commonly shared knowledge is essential for effective language use.

claims.text: The current political polarization over education curricula is driven by party spirit rather than evidence-based efforts to improve equity.
claims.source_passage: Current political polarization of this issue reflects now chiefly a spirit of party rather than of hard thought about achieving higher equity and a better-educated citizenry.

claims.text: Antonio Gramsci's concept of the 'common school' was intended as a school of general culture necessary for reasons of equal opportunity.
claims.source_passage: the 'common school' Gramsci was thinking of was 'a school of humanistic formation.' The context makes it clear that for Gramsci the 'common school' is necessary for 'reasons of equal opportunity'

claims.text: The 'common school' concept was a foundational element in building American democracy.
claims.source_passage: The 'common school' is, of course, the very phrase and concept that helped build American democracy.

claims.text: The early curriculum (grades preK–5) requires hard, specific content and commonality across schools.
claims.source_passage: The early curriculum requires hard, specific thought, and commonality in grades preK–5.

claims.text: Both the left and the right have been misled by the theory of providential individualism in education.
claims.source_passage: Both the left and the right have been misled by the theory of providential individualism in education.

claims.text: Individualized curriculum in the early grades disables individuals rather than helping them.
claims.source_passage: Individualism in the early grades disables many individuals.

claims.text: Individualism and curricular incoherence are primary causes of the stagnation in narrowing achievement gaps in the US, Britain, France, and Sweden.
claims.source_passage: Individualism with its curricular incoherence has been a chief cause of stasis or worse in the effort to narrow the achievement gaps between racial, ethnic, and economic groups in the United States, Britain, France, and Sweden.

claims.text: Providential individualism is an outmoded intellectual residue from history, not a logical requirement of either left-wing or right-wing politics.
claims.source_passage: Providential individualism is an outmoded residue, a sedimentary deposit from intellectual history. It is not a logical nor a necessary element in the aims of either left or right.

claims.text: Equality of opportunity cannot be achieved through an individualized curriculum in the early grades.
claims.source_passage: If equality of opportunity remains a primary aim, both parties will need to disavow the impossible dream of an effective individualized curriculum in the early grades.

claims.text: Differentiated curricula based on tracking is negatively associated with student performance and reduces exposure to advanced content.
claims.source_passage: Use of a differentiated curriculum based on tracking is negatively associated with student performance on the international assessments and also reduces opportunities for some students to be exposed to more advanced curriculum.

claims.text: The relevance of French educational data to the United States is supported by the fact that France has a diverse population equivalent in size to California and Texas combined.
claims.source_passage: France has a diverse population as big as that of California and Texas combined. We need to take note.

claims.text: In the pre-1989 French system, educational publishers competed on price and presentation effectiveness because they were prohibited from controlling the core content of schooling.
claims.source_passage: The publishers had no control over the basic content of schooling; they gained their competitive advantage by presenting the content attractively and effectively at a good price.

claims.text: Pierre Bourdieu's own academic success, having risen from a disadvantaged background where standard French was not his home language, serves as a counter-example to his theory of social reproduction.
claims.source_passage: Bourdieu himself had grown up under disadvantage; Standard French was not his home language. No mere social reproduction there! Why dismantle a school system that had produced a Bourdieu?

claims.text: Progressive education correctly identifies that child interest and activity are fundamental to learning, but it is insufficient because it lacks a cohesive theory of social welfare.
claims.source_passage: Progressive Education . . . has focused attention squarely upon the child; it has recognized the fundamental importance of the interest of the learner; it has defended the thesis that activity lies at the root of all true education . . . it has championed the rights of the child as a free personality. All of this is excellent; but in my judgment it is not enough. The great weakness of Progressive Education lies in the fact that it has elaborated no theory of social welfare.

claims.text: The depoliticizing of primary education, by moving away from partisan-driven individualism, is a primary practical objective for achieving educational equity.
claims.source_passage: The depoliticizing of primary education could be the most important practical theme of this book.

claims.text: Teacher union support for individualized education is a result of indoctrination in teacher-training institutes rather than an inherent ideological requirement of labor organization.
claims.source_passage: But teacher unions are not inherently of that view. Their membership has simply been indoctrinated by their teacher-training institutions, which are theological institutes for progressivism.

claims.text: The old French school system was rooted in Napoleon's meritocratic principle of 'a career open to talents' and the egalitarian ideals of the 1790 Condorcet plan.
claims.source_passage: The old French centralized system with a common curriculum was not, as many on the French left imagined, a product of hidebound right-wing politics, but of the old egalitarian left, starting with Condorcet’s plan of 1790, A Common Education for Children, and Napoleon’s meritocratic principle of 'a career open to talents.'

claims.text: Education colleges redefined the role of a teacher as a 'resource person' or 'guide on the side' to move away from direct instructional models.
claims.source_passage: Education colleges required a teacher to be called a 'resource person,' a guide on the side. A class lesson was to be considered a 'session,' not a lecture.

claims.text: The failure to transmit national culture through a common heritage results in academic failure and eventual social explosion/violence.
claims.source_passage: It is with them in mind that we renounced teaching the culture of our country and transmitting to all a common heritage. Today, those young people have twice the average risk of serious academic failure. Who can fail to see that that is the true violence; that such denial of transmission cannot fail, one day, to explode?

evidence.description: 2007 French Ministry of Education data compiled by Paola Mattei showing achievement decline and increased inequity.
evidence.source_reference: French Ministry of Education, Note d’Information 08-38 (2008)

evidence.description: SAT verbal score data showing a 0.5 standard deviation drop from 1960 to 1980.
evidence.source_reference: William W. Turnbull (1985); Donald P. Hayes et al. (1996)

evidence.description: The Iowa Test of Educational Development data showing a drop of almost one-third of a standard deviation (1965-1975) despite stable demographics.
A 1936 March of Time newsreel titled 'New Schools for Old' documenting the rise of progressive public schools in the US.
National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) verbal scores for seventeen-year-olds from 1971 showing flat results.
Stable pupil demographics in Iowa and Minnesota coinciding with test score declines, ruling out demographic change as a causal factor.
evidence.source_reference: L. A. Munday, Declining Admissions Test Scores (1976)

evidence.description: The French National Curriculum (pre-1989), a ~100-page document specifying minutes per week for disciplines and specific grade-level topics.
Figure 7.1: A chart from 1991 showing literacy levels for advantaged and disadvantaged ninth graders by country, highlighting the success of France and Sweden and the failure of Germany.
evidence.source_reference: Binkley, Rust, and Williams, Reading Literacy in an International Perspective (1997)

evidence.description: Preschool attendance statistics in France showing nearly 100% attendance for four- and five-year-olds.
The Bourdieu-Gros Report, which advocated for a pluralistic, multicultural approach and emphasized general skills over facts.
The verbatim text of the 1989 Loi Jospin preamble regarding student-led curriculum.
President Valéry Giscard d’Estaing's 1977 declaration on the unifying role of the common curriculum.
The Preamble of the 1989 French Education Law (Loi Jospin), which defines education as child-centered and a right to personality development.
evidence.source_reference: Loi d'orientation sur l'éducation n° 89-486 du 10 juillet 1989

evidence.description: Statistical disparities in French university major choices and cultural tastes correlating with social class.
The rising number of primary students unable to read or reckon, leading to grade repetition.
evidence.source_reference: Marc Le Bris, 'L'École Primaire: Les Origines de l'Échec Scolaire'

evidence.description: Bourdieu's statistical data on the correlation between university major choice and social class.
evidence.source_passage: By a clear statistical margin these decisions about a major study area were class based. Bourdieu also analyzed the different tastes in music and movies of university students, and found statistically significant correlations with class.

evidence.description: The specific pedagogical principles and lack of citation in the official Bourdieu-Gros Report.
evidence.source_passage: Yet to read the Bourdieu-Gros Report, which became the basis for new 1989 law, is to be struck by its vagueness regarding the curriculum... the report defended the hoary progressive idea of teaching critical-thinking skills... That statement, assumed to be self-evident, is not backed up by any footnotes.

evidence.description: Historical origins of the French curriculum in the Enlightenment and Napoleonic eras.
evidence.source_passage: The old French centralized system... starting with Condorcet’s plan of 1790, A Common Education for Children, and Napoleon’s meritocratic principle of “a career open to talents.”

evidence.description: French Ministry of Education periodic 'soundings' or longitudinal data collection on student competencies.
evidence.source_passage: The French Ministry of Education takes periodic “soundings” of the public school system, recording in detail the level of student competencies, along with demographic information about the students.

evidence.description: Baseline sounding of French student achievement at the end of fifth grade conducted by the French Ministry of Education.
Comparative soundings using identical test instruments in 1997 and 2007, involving over 4,000 fifth graders and 300,000 responses.
Figure 7.2 illustrating the drop in z-scores for literacy among children of different social classes (unemployed vs. professionals) between 1987 and 2007.
DEPP Note d'Information 08-38: A twenty-year study showing the performance of CM2 students in reading, writing, and arithmetic between 1987 and 2007.
evidence.source_passage: As the French data from 1987 to 2007 show, the new 1990 arrangements greatly intensified the social reproduction they were supposed to diminish.

evidence.description: The 'Journal of a Clandestine Teacher', an account of ideological conformity and anti-knowledge methods in French teacher training.
evidence.source_reference: Rachel Boutonnet, Journal d'une Institutrice Clandestine (2003)
evidence.source_passage: She was attending one of the new education schools... so outraged by the boredom, misinformation, and ideological conformity thrust upon her that she resolved to keep a journal.

evidence.description: A bestselling book detailing the persistent failure of French schools and the long-term dominance of progressive methods in normal schools.
evidence.source_reference: Marc Le Bris, Et Vos Enfants ne Sauront pas Lire (2004)
evidence.source_passage: For twenty years, the National Education system has prevented me from doing my job. When I left the normal school in 1977, I was a young progressive teacher...

evidence.description: Evidence that teacher entry standards and quality remained high and constant throughout the period of student performance decline.
evidence.source_reference: Lafforgue and Lurçat, eds., La Débâcle de l’École (2007)
evidence.source_passage: “Teacher quality” remained constant in France by all objective measures during the period of decline.

evidence.description: Marc Le Bris's book detailing his experience as a 'progressive' teacher whose students suffered until he returned to traditional methods.
evidence.source_reference: Marc Le Bris, Your Children Won’t Know How to Read or Count (2004)

evidence.description: The findings and position statement of GRIP (Groupe de Recherches Interdisciplinaires sur les Programmes) regarding curricular disintegration.
evidence.source_reference: Groupe de Recherches Interdisciplinaires sur les Programmes (GRIP)

evidence.description: International PISA rankings in 2012 showing France's decline in academic equity.
François-Xavier Bellamy's deconstruction of Bourdieu's influence on the lack of transmission in schools.
evidence.source_reference: Francois-Xavier Bellamy, Les Désherités ou l’Urgence de Transmettre (2014)

evidence.description: A 2014 report by Professor Leif Lewin commissioned by the Swedish government analyzing the effects of decentralization.
evidence.source_passage: In 2014, after a decade of the experiment, the Swedish government commissioned a report from Professor Leif Lewin regarding the effects of its decentralization of Swedish schools

evidence.description: PISA verbal scores for Sweden showing a drop from 516 in 2000 to 483 in 2012.
evidence.source_passage: Between 2000 and 2012, Swedish PISA verbal scores fell from 516... to 483... It is the largest drop ever recorded by PISA.

evidence.description: The Swedish Education Act's definition of the 'equivalence' principle.
evidence.source_reference: Swedish Ministry of Education summary of the Education Act
evidence.source_passage: The Education Act stipulates that the education should be adapted to each pupil’s circumstances and needs

evidence.description: Historical account of the 1994 Swedish education reform replacing the common curriculum.
evidence.source_reference: Swedish Ministry of Education (1994 Education Act)
evidence.source_passage: A principle of “equivalence” was legislated to replace the common curriculum, summarized as follows by the Swedish Ministry of Education: “Education should be adapted to each pupil’s circumstances and needs... Irrespective of where in the country it is provided... equivalent education does not mean that the education should be the same everywhere.”

evidence.description: Expert explanation of Germany's educational improvement being linked to curriculum alignment.
evidence.source_reference: Manfred Prenzel, head of PISA studies in Germany
evidence.source_passage: The post-Schock curriculum guides put out by the Länder... are now much more aligned with each other, and are more specific grade by grade.

evidence.description: Swedish government policy statement emphasizing education adapted to individual background and circumstances.
The Plowden Report (1967), which encouraged informal, child-centered education in the UK.
The Bennett Report (1976), which linked progressive methods to exacerbated inequalities.
evidence.source_reference: Neville Bennett, Teaching Styles and Pupil Progress (1976)

evidence.description: W. H. Auden's poem 'Doggerel by a Senior Citizen,' questioning the success of educational permissiveness.
The American Federation of Teachers' (AFT) long-term support for the Core Knowledge Curriculum.
Correspondence from Gramsci scholar Joseph Buttigieg regarding the 'unitary school.'
The Medrich report on international mathematics and science assessment linking differentiated curriculum to negative performance outcomes.
evidence.source_reference: Elliott A. Medrich, International Mathematics and Science Assessment (1992)

evidence.description: A 2010 Swedish study concluding that individualized learning over teacher instruction explained the drop in student performance.
evidence.source_reference: Swedish study published in 2010 cited in The Economist

concepts.author_definition: A term for catastrophic failure, borrowed from the French military defeat of 1940, applied here to the systemic failure of the French school system following the 1989 reforms.
Educational strategies that prioritize generic cognitive skills and tailored content over a communal, knowledge-rich curriculum.
A 'virus-like' system of ideas, spanning political lines, that assumes natural developmental paths or individualized choice will inherently lead to optimal educational outcomes.
A rigorous classroom exercise where students transcribe spoken text with perfect spelling and punctuation to master standard written language.
concepts.source_passage: the dreaded dictée, under which students had to write down what the teacher spoke with perfect spelling and punctuation in the standard written forms of the French language.

concepts.author_definition: A school system characterized by a well-developed, common, and specific national curriculum delivered uniformly to all children.
The 1989 French educational law that decentralized the school system, prioritized individual child development, and shifted focus from a national common curriculum to school-level autonomy.
The 'platform' of national standards established by the Jospin law, which the author describes as vague and practically non-existent regarding specific content.
A pejorative term used by French reformers to describe the 'piling up of facts' in the traditional curriculum, which they sought to replace with 'all-purpose skills.'
A French version of 'progressive education' originating in the 1960s that places the individual child at the center and favors natural development over rote memorization.
concepts.source_passage: They had been instructed... in a new point of view—that of la nouvelle education, which we call “progressive education.”

concepts.term: La méthode globale / La méthode mixte
concepts.author_definition: Naturalistic reading methods (equivalent to 'whole language' or 'balanced literacy') that avoid explicit phonics and are blamed for causing reading failure.
concepts.source_passage: The new naturalistic theories into which French teachers were being indoctrinated—for example, in la méthode globale, and la méthode mixte in reading

concepts.author_definition: Pierre Bourdieu's theory that education systems reinforce existing social hierarchies rather than providing a ladder for mobility.
concepts.source_passage: His radical claim: French schooling was a means of reproducing social class, not overcoming it.

concepts.author_definition: The belief that logic, habits of thought, and cognitive tools are independent of specific content and can be applied universally to tasks like reading.
concepts.common_alternative: Transferable soft skills or higher-order thinking skills.
concepts.source_passage: elementary logic, and, by the acquisition of habits of thought, the techniques and cognitive tools which are indispensable in conducting reasoning that is rigorous and thoughtful—those same general competences are required in the reading of texts.

concepts.author_definition: Periodic national assessments of the public school system recording student competencies and demographic information to track the effects of policy.
The specific academic knowledge and vocabulary of the public sphere that allows for success on literacy tests and social mobility.
A term borrowed from Roger Shattuck used as a metaphor for the French reform's removal of common knowledge from the curriculum.
A teaching method that claims to combine phonics with naturalistic whole-language approaches but is characterized here as essentially naturalistic and ineffective.
concepts.common_alternative: A compromise between whole language and phonics.

concepts.author_definition: The process where a shared national curriculum is broken down or made random, compromising the ability of students to gain necessary academic knowledge.
The active passing on of a country's culture and common heritage to the next generation through the school system.
Students (particularly from low-income or immigrant backgrounds) who are deprived of social mobility because schools no longer transmit the cultural capital required for success.
A policy framework where the national government dictates a common curriculum and allocates extra resources to low-achieving students to ensure uniform outcomes across all schools.
A principle in the 1994 Swedish law that replaced uniform curricula with the requirement that education be adapted to each individual pupil's background, experiences, and needs.
The naive belief that allowing children (or markets) to follow their natural, unguided inclinations will automatically lead to positive social and educational outcomes.
The national scandal and period of stock-taking in Germany after PISA reported surprisingly poor student scores in 2000, leading to major curricular reforms.
The historical left-wing principle of offering every child the exact same educational opportunities and content in the early years to ensure social equity.
concepts.source_passage: the left has favored universalism—that is, the idea of offering each child the very same educational opportunities in the early years.

concepts.author_definition: An educational approach, currently favored by both right-wing market advocates and left-wing progressive educators, that tailors curriculum to the specific background and perceived needs of the individual child.
concepts.source_passage: Education should be adapted to each pupil’s circumstances and needs, based on the pupils’ background, earlier experiences, language, and knowledge.

concepts.author_definition: Antonio Gramsci's concept of a common school focused on humanistic formation and general culture, intended to empower poor children.
Napoleon's meritocratic principle that helped form the basis of the egalitarian French centralized school system.
A progressive educational term for a teacher as a 'resource person' rather than a direct lecturer.
An all-purpose skill emphasized by progressive teacher training at the expense of specific content acquisition.
The foundational concept of American democracy, also cited by Gramsci, requiring a unitary school of general culture to ensure equal opportunity.

cases.name: France post-1989
cases.description: Following the 1989 loi Jospin, France transitioned from a top-performing, equitable national curriculum system to an individualized, skill-based system, resulting in a documented decline in achievement and equity.
key_passages: The data showed another educational system that, like our own, had adopted skill-based, individualized, educational principles, and subsequently declined from being among the best and most equitable large school systems in the world to being one of the worst and least equitable.

cases.name: United States 1960-1980
cases.description: The period where American verbal test scores (SAT, ACT, and state tests) plummeted significantly following the universal adoption of 1930s progressive curriculum reforms.
key_passages: SAT verbal scores had plummeted half a standard deviation in the twenty years between 1960 and 1980.
The origins of our decline can be clearly traced to our having made similar intellectual mistakes half a century before.

cases.name: France (pre-1989)
cases.description: A period of French educational history characterized by a centralized national curriculum and high preschool attendance. During this time, France achieved the highest average student achievement and greatest equity among large, diverse nations, successfully educating its immigrant population better than its neighbors.
key_passages: In the 1980s, France could celebrate (with Sweden) the best large school systems in Europe.
France exhibited the highest average student achievement with the greatest equity of any large, diverse country. It educated immigrants better than any other European nation.

cases.name: The Loi Jospin (1989 Reform)
cases.description: A major legislative shift in French education that decentralized the curriculum. Though implemented suddenly in 1989, the author argues its intellectual roots (progressive ideas) had been brewing in teacher training schools since the 1960s. This shift is characterized as the start of a catastrophic decline in French performance.
key_passages: Then, after 1989, things changed radically... the intellectual preparation for the catastrophic loi Jospin of 1989 had been brewing in French education schools since the 1960s.
Because French schooling was centrally controlled, it decentralized itself in 1989 in a wrenching, centralized manner.

cases.name: The 1989 French Educational Reform
cases.description: A radical structural and philosophical shift in the French education system. Initiated by Education Minister Lionel Jospin and based on the Bourdieu-Gros Report, the reform abandoned the centuries-old tradition of a uniform national curriculum (intended to unify society and reduce inequality) in favor of decentralization, school autonomy, child-centered curriculum design, and an emphasis on general skills over specific subject matter knowledge.
key_passages: In 1989, France completely overthrew that tradition by passing a law saying in effect that each child would go to a different primary school and middle school.
The structure of the radical change was to be based on a new report called the Bourdieu-Gros Report.
The emphasis was to be on skills, not facts... that all-purpose skills are far more important than the piling up of knowledge.
contestable_aspects: The causal link between these specific legal changes and later educational decline
The interpretation of the 'socle' as nonspecific

cases.name: France's 1989 Educational Reform (Loi Jospin)
cases.description: A radical shift in French policy that abandoned the national curriculum in favor of localized, individualized instruction. It was based on the Bourdieu-Gros report and sought to make the system more egalitarian by focusing on child-centered learning and all-purpose skills.
key_passages: The very preamble of the new 1989 law made clear that French education was not going to be “lockstep” any longer. It was going to be child-centered.
Thenceforth each primary school was to produce its own curriculum.

cases.name: May 1968 Student Revolts
cases.description: Massive student-led protests in France that criticized established institutions, including the traditional school system, creating the political urgency for the 1989 curricular changes.
key_passages: a demand that had continued to animate French politics ever since the alarming student revolts of May 1968. These revolts criticized almost all of France’s established institutions.

cases.name: The French Curriculum Shift 1987–2007
cases.description: France shifted from a centralized, knowledge-based national curriculum to an individualized, skill-centered curriculum following the 1989 Jospin law. The outcome was a significant drop in national test scores and an increase in achievement gaps between social classes, documented by identical tests administered over twenty years.
key_passages: In 1987, the French Ministry had taken one of its periodic national soundings... Those 1987 data are the reference points for tracing the subsequent effects of the child-centered skills curriculum that began to be put in place in 1990.
The child-centered skills curriculum had caused the average of literacy scores to fall four-tenths of a standard deviation.
The new 1990 arrangements greatly intensified the social reproduction they were supposed to diminish.

cases.name: France's PISA Performance 1987-2012
cases.description: France transitioned from being a leader in educational equity to the most inegalitarian country in the PISA rankings following the 1989 Loi Jospin reforms.
key_passages: France, of all countries in the world, has deteriorated the most in the equity of its schools.
It was a stunning reversal after being at the top in the equity of its results!

cases.name: Sweden 1994 Reform
cases.description: Sweden transitioned from a highly egalitarian, centralized system with a common national curriculum to a decentralized system emphasizing individualization and market competition (school choice). The reform gave local schools the authority to determine knowledge content. The result was a significant decline in national PISA scores and a widening of the socio-economic achievement gap.
key_passages: In 2014, after a decade of the experiment... Lewin showed that decentralization had caused a severe drop in students’ abilities in reading, writing, and arithmetic.
Under the new curricula, the state largely abandoned the ambition to determine the knowledge content of the school, and transferred that determination to local schools.
Between 2000 and 2012, Swedish PISA verbal scores fell from 516... to 483... the largest drop ever recorded by PISA.
contestable_aspects: The role of market competition vs. the role of curriculum changes in the decline

cases.name: Sweden Post-1994
cases.description: Sweden moved from a highly centralized, equitable system to one focused on individualization and thinking skills in 1994. The result was a dramatic drop in PISA scores and a significant increase in social inequality, making it one of the fastest-deteriorating systems in the developed world.
key_passages: The new law of 1994 discarded those arrangements.
In the primary grades, the coherence of the common curriculum disappeared.
Now in the PISA studies Sweden is second only to France as the country that has deteriorated the most in social equality.

cases.name: Germany Post-PISA Shock
cases.description: After scoring poorly in 2000 (below the US), Germany reacted to 'der PISA Schock' by adopting well-defined, grade-specific curricula aligned across its states (Länder). This led to continuous improvement in achievement and equity through 2012.
key_passages: Germany is an object lesson in the other direction.
Reacting to the Schock, Germany began to adopt well-defined curricula.
After that it began to score above average for developed nations.

cases.name: 1960s-1970s Great Britain
cases.description: Under a Labor government, the Plowden Report institutionalized progressive, child-centered education. This led to a contentious period where critics blamed these methods for educational failings, and later reports suggested they worsened inequality.
key_passages: In 1967... Britain had issued the Plowden Report under the auspices of a left-leaning Labor government.
The Bennett Report of 1976 argued that the data showed that the new methods were exacerbating inequalities without leading to any improvement in 'creativity.'

thinkers.name: Laurent Lafforgue
thinkers.key_idea: The 1989 French school reforms were a 'débâcle' and a 'misunderstood tragedy' caused by avoidable intellectual mistakes by elites.
thinkers.source_work: La Débâcle de l’École: Une Tragédie Incomprise (2007)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Lafforgue's historical analogy to the 1940 military defeat to frame the gravity of the French educational decline.
key_passages: Lafforgue... His view, which I agree with, is that top French intellectuals made big avoidable mistakes in 1989.

thinkers.name: Paola Mattei
thinkers.key_idea: Compiling and analyzing the 2007 French Ministry of Education data that quantified the decline of the French system.
thinkers.source_work: Web data / Secular Institutions, Islam and Education Policy (2016)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch credits Mattei's dissemination of French data as the primary catalyst for writing the book.
key_passages: I first decided to write this book after I ran across the astonishing 2007 data compiled by the French Ministry of Education, recently placed on the Web by Paola Mattei.

thinkers.name: Valéry Giscard d’Estaing
thinkers.key_idea: The defining purpose of French schooling is to impart a common culture to all children to reduce inequality.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites the former President's 1977 statement as the definitive articulation of the communal school model that he advocates.
key_passages: President Giscard d’Estaing in 1977, who stated the basic idea of French schooling: to enable all French children to possess a common culture.

thinkers.name: Pierre Bourdieu
thinkers.key_idea: The national curriculum was non-egalitarian because it favored the culture that elite children already possessed, thereby excluding children from other backgrounds.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch characterizes Bourdieu's ideas as the intellectual justification for dismantling the communal curriculum that previously promoted equality.
key_passages: Pierre Bourdieu had been chosen to head the committee... because he had been in the forefront of scholars who complained that the old-fashioned national curriculum was not as egalitarian as it claimed to be.

thinkers.name: Lionel Jospin
thinkers.key_idea: Educational reform should decentralize power, allow for local autonomy in subject matter, and focus on the individual learning styles and natural development of students.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch presents Jospin as the political architect of the reform that imported 'American' progressive failures into France.
key_passages: Jospin turned to the matter of curriculum, he observed that subject matter would be determined by each district and school under its own plan.

thinkers.name: Jean-Claude Passeron
thinkers.key_idea: Co-authored the critique that French schooling perpetuates class repression.
thinkers.engagement: Mentioned as Bourdieu's collaborator in the social analysis that influenced the 1989 reforms.
key_passages: Bourdieu and his coauthor Jean-Claude Passeron claimed that French schooling was not as egalitarian as it conceived itself to be.

thinkers.name: François Gros
thinkers.key_idea: Co-authored the 1989 report arguing that all-purpose skills are more vital than knowledge accumulation.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites his report as the specific intellectual blueprint for the shift to skill-centrism.
key_passages: Gros Report that all-purpose skills are far more important than the piling up of knowledge.

thinkers.name: Condorcet
thinkers.key_idea: A plan for a common education for all children to ensure equality.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Condorcet to prove that centralized common curricula are products of egalitarian, not right-wing, philosophy.
key_passages: starting with Condorcet’s plan of 1790, A Common Education for Children

thinkers.name: Marc Le Bris
